vulnerabilities.description: XSS vulnerability: dangerouslySetInnerHTML renders unsanitized HTML from marked.parse(). The 'marked' library does not escape HTML by default, allowing script injection through markdown content.

Prototype Pollution vulnerability: Object spread merging ({ ...fallback, ...parsed }) can inject malicious prototype properties (__proto__, constructor, prototype) from localStorage JSON, potentially compromising application behavior.
No input validation: Keys and values are stored directly without sanitization. If malicious data is stored, it could be exploited when loaded and used in other parts of the codebase.
Limited sanitization scope: While sanitize() validates structure, it doesn't protect against all potential prototype pollution vectors in arrays or nested objects.

recommendations: HIGH PRIORITY: Add HTML sanitization to markdown rendering. Use DOMPurify or configure marked with an extension that escapes HTML: `marked.use({ breaks: true }); marked.setOptions({ pedantic: false, gfm: true, hooks: { preprocess: (markdown) => sanitize(markdown) } })`
HIGH PRIORITY: Fix prototype pollution in store.ts. Use Object.assign() with null prototype or use a safe merge utility: `const safe = Object.create(null); Object.assign(safe, fallback, parsed);`

MEDIUM PRIORITY: Add input validation to memory.js. Validate keys match a whitelist pattern (e.g., alphanumeric + underscore) before storing: `if (!/^[a-z0-9_]+$/i.test(key)) throw new Error('Invalid key')`
MEDIUM PRIORITY: Upgrade 'marked' dependency and verify it's configured to not render raw HTML. Add a test that verifies markdown with script tags renders as text, not executable code.
LOW PRIORITY: Extend sanitize() in progress.ts to handle nested objects recursively and validate all array elements are of expected type.
LOW PRIORITY: Audit all localStorage reads in the codebase to ensure they use the sanitize() function consistently.
